narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes a Year 3 school decile of 2, which is below the typical student's 5.5 average, a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30 that's actually a bit above the 3.21 average, an LSAT score of 36.50 very close to the usual 36.70, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time like most applicants. The model's assessment gave you a 52.7% predicted chance of failing the bar exam on your first try, which tipped the scales just enough to reject your application—law schools use this prediction to prioritize students likely to pass and succeed in practice. The biggest factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your lower Year 3 decile ranking, as it stood out as weaker compared to what the model sees in students who typically pass. Your Year 1 decile of 3 also contributed somewhat toward the risk, being below average as well. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA strongly pulled in the opposite direction, signaling potential for success since it's higher than most. Your LSAT score, nearly spot-on with averages, also helped nudge the prediction toward passing, as did your upper-middle family income and full-time enrollment plans, which align with successful profiles. Overall, while some of your academic rankings from school years were the most influential drags compared to typical admitted students, your other strengths like GPA and LSAT kept the risk from being overwhelming. This isn't a judgment on your worth or potential—it's just one model's snapshot based on patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your school deciles, preparing even more rigorously for the LSAT if possible, and gathering letters highlighting your resilience as a Black woman in law; many schools value holistic reviews beyond predictions. You have the drive—reach out to advisors for appeal options or alternative paths, and keep pushing; your story deserves to be told in the courtroom. We're rooting for you.
